title: Accordion fold journal insert, making an expanding section
subtitle: How to fold and attach an accordion-pleated strip as an insert or timeline
excerpt: An accordion-folded strip sewn or tipped into a journal creates an expanding section that holds more material than the page count suggests. It can function as a timeline, a map fold, a panoramic drawing surface, or a sequence of smaller pockets. The fold itself takes only minutes to produce from a single long strip of paper.

glossaryTerms.definition: A paper folding technique in which a strip is folded back and forth in alternating directions, producing a pleated structure that expands and collapses. Also called a concertina fold or leporello.

The two directions in paper folding. A valley fold produces a groove (the crease is on top and the fold goes away from you); a mountain fold produces a ridge (the crease is underneath and the fold comes toward you).

content.text: is one of the simplest ways to add hidden surface area to a journal. A strip folded into eight panels provides seven usable panels when one end is used as the attachment flap. The fold is precise when scored: guessing the fold position by eye produces uneven panels that do not collapse flat.

items.name: Long paper strip, 90-120 gsm
items.notes: Cut to the journal page height and as long as needed for the panel count. Lightweight cartridge paper works well; avoid heavy card, which resists repeated folding.

items.name: Bone folder
items.notes: For scoring and creasing each panel fold precisely.

items.name: Metal ruler, 30 cm
items.notes: For marking consistent panel widths and guiding the bone folder.

items.name: Craft knife and cutting mat
items.notes: For trimming the strip to length.

items.name: PVA, acid-free
items.notes: Optional, for attaching the first or last panel to the journal page as the attachment flap.

content.text: Mark and score the panel divisions

Decide on the panel width, equal to the journal page width produces a panel that opens to exactly double-page width. Mark each panel division along the top edge of the strip lightly in pencil. Lay the metal ruler along each mark and score a fold line with the bone folder across the full strip width. Score firmly enough to compress the paper fibres without cutting through.
Fold along the first scored line toward you (a

). Fold the next scored line away from you (mountain fold). Continue alternating to the end of the strip. Press each fold firmly with the bone folder before moving to the next. Collapse the whole strip flat and press under a weight for ten minutes to set the folds.

The accordion can be attached at one end only (so it hangs from the page and expands away from it) or at both ends (so it is anchored between two pages and the panels open between them). For single-end attachment, apply a thin line of PVA to the last panel and press it to the journal page; weight until dry. For double-end attachment, use a folded paper hinge on each end rather than direct adhesive, a hinge allows the accordion to flex without stressing the attachment point.
